content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Chapter One: The Hour Between Yesterday and Tomorrow
Most people slept through the muted hours.
The city certainly did.
London would quieten, traffic would thin, and the glow from office towers would fade into the darkness. Trains carried their final passengers home. Shop shutters rolled down. Streets emptied.
Yet there was always someone awake.
Tonight, that someone was seventeen-year-old Fezzim Charlie.
I sat at a worn desk in a cramped flat above a corner shop in South London, staring out at the amber streetlights beyond my window. Rain traced crooked paths down the glass while distant sirens drifted through the night.
My bedroom was hardly impressive.
The wallpaper peeled at the corners. The radiator rattled whenever it felt like working. The ceiling carried a faint water stain shaped suspiciously like Scotland.
Still, I loved the room.
It was where impossible things were planned.
Open exercise books covered his desk. Pages overflowed with business ideas, clothing sketches, marketing plans, logos, and numbers scribbled in every available margin.
At the top of nearly every page were the same two words.
Muted Hours.
The name had come to me during one sleepless night months ago.Most successful people spoke about opportunities.I thought opportunities were overrated.What really mattered were the hours nobody saw.
The early mornings.
The late nights.
The sacrifices.
The quiet work.
The moments when everyone else had gone home and given up.Those were the muted hours.
And one day, I intended to build something great from them.My mates laughed whenever I spoke about becoming wealthy. Not because they disliked me. Because they thought everyone dreamed of becoming rich. Very few actually did anything about it. I was determined to be different.
I wasn’t interested in flashy sports cars or showing off online.
I wanted freedom.
I wanted to help his mum stop counting every pound before going shopping.
I wanted to prove that where you started didn’t decide where you finished.Most of all, I wanted Muted Hours to become a name recognised across the country. Perhaps even the world. The thought made me smile. Then my laptop chimed. A notification. “At half-past two in the morning?” I frowned.
“Nobody sensible was messaging me at this hour.” I clicked.
The sender’s name was blank. The message contained only six words.
Your dream already knows your name.
I stared at the screen. A cold sensation crept up my spine. Before I could react, another message appeared.
Tomorrow. Midnight. Don’t be late.
Attached below was a single photograph. My smile vanished. The image showed a luxury black bookmark resting on a wooden table. Embossed in silver at its centre was a familiar logo.
Muted Hours.
The exact design from my notebook. The notebook nobody had ever seen
I didn’t get much sleep after the message. Not because I thought it was supernatural. Because it was strange. Someone had somehow found a logo I’d never posted online.
That bothered me.
The next morning, I opened my notebook before leaving for college. Every sketch was still there. Every page looked untouched. I took a photo of the logo and headed out.
College was the usual mix of lectures, deadlines, and people pretending they knew what they were doing with their lives.
I was one of those people.
While everyone else discussed assignments during lunch, I sat in the corner of the canteen working on Muted Hours.
The idea had started as a clothing brand.
Then a lifestyle brand.
Then a notebook brand.
Then a website.
Then a clothing brand again!The truth was, I wasn’t entirely sure what it was yet. I only knew one thing. I wanted to build something. Something with my name behind it. Something that wouldn’t disappear the moment I graduated.
That afternoon, I showed the anonymous message to my best mate, Tjay.He stared at the screen.
“That’s creepy.”
“Helpful.”
“I’m serious.”
“So am I.”
He zoomed in on the image.
The bookmark looked expensive. Black leather. Silver lettering. Professional photography. Far more professional than anything I’d made.
Then he pointed at the bottom corner. “What’s that?”
I leaned closer.
There was a faint watermark. A website address. Tiny enough to miss at first glance. My heart started racing. For the first time, I had an actual lead. After classes, I rushed home and typed the website into my laptop. The page loaded instantly. A small design studio based in Manchester.Nothing unusual. Until I clicked on their portfolio. There it was. My logo. Muted Hours. Not exactly my logo. But close enough that anyone would think they came from the same designer.
I sat frozen. Someone else had the same idea. The same name. The same aesthetic. For months I’d convinced myself that Muted Hours was unique. Now I wasn’t so sure. I spent the next two hours researching. The company wasn’t famous. Their social media pages barely had any followers. Their online shop wasn’t even live yet. Still, seeing the name somewhere else felt like discovering someone had borrowed a piece of my future.
At dinner, Mum noticed something was wrong.
“You’ve barely touched your food.”
“I’m fine.”
“You look like you’ve just failed an exam.”
I laughed.
“Nothing that dramatic.”
She folded her arms.
“Try me.”
I hesitated.
Then I explained everything.
The brand, the message, the website, the fear that I’d wasted months building something that already existed.
Mum listened quietly.
When I finished, she shrugged.
“Good.”
I blinked.
“Good?”
“Yes, good.”
“How is this good?”
She smiled.
“Because now you know it’s a real idea.”
I frowned.
“What do you mean?”
“If somebody else believes it’s worth building, maybe it is.”
For a moment, neither of us spoke.
Then she added:
“The question isn’t whether someone had the idea first.”
“It’s whether you’ll do more with it.”
That night, I returned to my room and opened my notebook. For the first time, I stopped drawing logos. Instead, I wrote a simple question across the top of a blank page.
What makes Muted Hours different?
Outside, rain tapped against the window. Inside, I started writing. And for the first time, the dream felt less like a fantasy and more like a plan.
